project_overview.project_name: COVID-19 Relief Effort for Afghan Communities and Households (REACH)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To provide emergency support to selected households through communities in project areas during the COVID-19 outbreak
project_overview.total_financing: US$280 million (IDA: US$155 million grant; ARTF: US$125 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 3.3 million households (5.1 million households combined with CCAP) in rural, peri-urban, and urban areas; targeting households with incomes of US$2 per day or lower (approximately 90% of population in targeted areas)

primary_locations: 14 provincial capital cities (Qala-I-Naw, Mazar-i-Sharif, Bamyan, Chaghcharan, Hirat, Kandahar, Mahmudi Raqi, Jalalabad, Zaranj, Paroon, Gardez, Bazarak, Chaharikar, Aybak)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national (approximately two-thirds of country; remaining one-third covered by CCAP)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is classified as fragile and conflict-affected; 47 hard-to-reach (HTR) districts with limited government access due to security concerns will be added in subsequent implementation phase; ongoing violent conflict between Taliban and government forces; active insurgent activity at record highs

key_components.component_name: Component 1: REACH Rural - Household Support in Rural and Peri-urban Areas
key_components.description: Provision of in-kind relief support (food staples and hygiene products) to approximately 2.2 million households in 234 rural districts and 19 provincial capital cities where CCAP is not operating. Single tranche of AFN 4,000 (approximately US$50 equivalent) per household. Three implementation modalities: reactivated NSP CDCs, ad-hoc CDCs, and ENETAWF-targeted CDCs with social mobilization.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: REACH Urban - Household Support in Provincial Capital Cities
key_components.description: Provision of in-kind and/or cash relief to approximately 450,000 households in 14 provincial capital cities. Two tranches of AFN 4,000 each (total US$100 equivalent). First tranche distributed in-kind or cash based on community preference and feasibility; second tranche to explore mobile money technology. Implementation through CDCs, Gozar Assemblies, or ad-hoc CDCs with support from facilitating partners.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: REACH Kabul - Household Support in Kabul Municipality
key_components.description: Provision of in-kind and/or cash relief to approximately 630,000 households (80% of total households) in Kabul Municipality. Two tranches of AFN 4,000 each (total US$100 equivalent). Implementation through ad-hoc Gozar Councils with support from Kabul Municipality, facilitating partners, and community representatives.

key_components.component_name: Component 4: Project Management, Communication, and Monitoring
key_components.description: Support for project management, capacity building, strategic communications campaign, and monitoring across three implementing agencies (MRRD, IDLG, KM). Leverages existing PIUs from CCAP and KMDP. Includes development of Management Information System (MIS), call centers for beneficiary surveys, communications strategy, and safeguard compliance monitoring.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Rural Rehabilitation and Development (MRRD) for rural areas; Independent Directorate of Local Governance (IDLG) for urban areas outside Kabul; Kabul Municipality (KM) for Kabul. Overall coordination through President-led inter-ministerial COVID-19 Response Task Force.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Coordination with Citizens' Charter Afghanistan Project (CCAP) to avoid duplication; coordination with humanitarian partners (UN agencies, NGOs) through Humanitarian Country Team (HCT); coordination with PAISA project for digital payment infrastructure; coordination with NSIA for household registry development; coordination with provincial governors and municipal mayors; coordination with Afghanistan Reconstruction Trust Fund (ARTF) donors.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Community-level procurement by CDCs/Gozar Assemblies/Ad-hoc CDCs for food packages using standardized contracts with pre-determined prices and quality standards. PIU-level procurement for facilitating partners, goods, and services. Fast-track emergency procurement procedures. Direct contracting and limited competition permitted. Thresholds increased for emergency response. Community procurement method applicable. Standard food basket defined at provincial level with flexibility for regional preferences.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Multi-layered monitoring: government monitoring at provincial, municipal, and national levels; community participatory monitoring (CPM) committees; facilitating partner monitoring and spot-checks; Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) monitoring at 20% of CDCs/Gozars; phone surveys of approximately 1,600 households/respondents per day; MIS-based data collection and tracking; grievance redress mechanism with call centers; beneficiary satisfaction surveys; results posted on government websites. Bank supervision and technical support.

identified_risks.risk_description: Power-sharing agreement between two factions disputing 2019 presidential election outcomes may lead to continued disputes, political instability, and administrative disruptions. Changes in key ministry staff and government restructuring could affect project implementation capacity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Direct World Bank technical support to all implementation areas; proven Bank capacity to deliver programs in difficult political contexts; high-level government coordination through President-led task force

identified_risks.risk_description: Continued insurgent activity and Taliban attacks at record highs despite recent US-Taliban peace agreement. Deterioration in security could divert government capacity, impede technical assistance, disrupt monitoring, and undermine project results. Limited government access to 47 hard-to-reach districts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Proven Bank capacity to monitor and support implementation in difficult security environments; coordination with humanitarian partners who have access to HTR areas; phased approach to HTR district coverage; use of facilitating partners with local presence

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial downside risks to Afghanistan's macroeconomic outlook. More severe and protracted COVID-19 impacts could reduce growth, government revenues, and international grant support. Grants equal 43% of GDP and finance 75% of public spending; current pledges expire December 2020. Potential fiscal shocks and financial sector weaknesses.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government's demonstrated capacity to maintain macroeconomic stability despite revenue declines; effective expenditure control; monetary policy framework maintenance; World Bank budget support operations

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan lacks a national social protection policy or system. Complex institutional arrangements with diverse implementation modalities across geographies. Food supply chain pressures, price inflation, and commodity availability risks. Possible delays in benefit delivery relative to crisis timeline.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Simple and narrow program objective; standardized design elements (same benefit package, pre-set prices, minimum delivery frequency, simple targeting); coordination with government on food supply maintenance; reliance on existing PIUs with additional support; contracted facilitating partners; temporary staff augmentation

identified_risks.risk_description: Unprecedented scale of nationwide emergency relief effort. Complexity of three different implementation modalities. Uncertainty around population numbers and household identification. Risk of delays in activity implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Leveraging existing CCAP systems and structures; use of proven community-driven development approach; simplified targeting methodology; standardized operations manual; MIS-based tracking; facilitating partner support; Bank technical assistance

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: MRRD, IDLG, and KM have shown control weaknesses in staff recruitment, fixed assets management, budget credibility, and internal audit. Limited experience with Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) standards. Capacity constraints in managing large-scale, rapid implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Leveraging existing PIUs from ongoing Bank projects; additional temporary staff and consultants (approximately 890 across three agencies); Bank-provided training and capacity building; enhanced fiduciary framework with additional controls; TPMA oversight; facilitating partner support; simplified implementation arrangements

identified_risks.risk_description: High fiduciary risk across Afghanistan portfolio. Large advance payments to CDCs/Gozars with delayed reconciliation (2.5-3 months). Weak financial reporting by community institutions. Beneficiary identification risks (no national registry). Procurement risks at community level. Distribution risks including possible omission of households, extortion, or misuse of cash. Allegations of fraud and corruption in portfolio.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Enhanced fiduciary framework with multiple controls: ex-ante controls in Operations Manual; use of technology (MIS with embedded controls, smartphones, geo-tagging); beneficiary identification requirements (Tazkira, mobile numbers); fixed-price food packages; community procurement guidelines; facilitating partner oversight; TPMA monitoring at 20% of sites; government monitoring at provincial/municipal/national levels; call center surveys; grievance redress mechanism; transparent communications campaign; third-party monitoring; external audit by Supreme Audit Office; prior Bank review of staffing and operating costs

identified_risks.risk_description: Project activities could contribute to COVID-19 spread through worker-community contact. Improper disposal of PPE could cause pollution. Social risks include potential for social exclusion, corruption, nepotism, and social conflict. Gender-based violence (GBV) and sexual exploitation/abuse (SEA) risks. Risks to people with disabilities. Security force management risks at distribution points. Crowd management challenges.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) to be prepared within 30 days of effectiveness; Labor Management Plan (LMP) with occupational health and safety (OHS) measures; COVID-19-specific protocols for social distancing and PPE use; Code of Conduct for all staff; GBV/SEA action plan; gender-sensitive training; female field staff (minimum 25%); targeted outreach to female-headed households; grievance redress mechanism with female-friendly channels; community participatory monitoring; security force management plan; crowd control protocols; door-to-door distribution as default modality; designated collection points with social distancing; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); robust communications campaign on eligibility and GRM

identified_risks.risk_description: Perceived unfairness of targeting could generate discontent and social conflict. Risk of elite capture of resources. Underlying social tensions and exclusion in conflict-affected context. Potential conflict between settled and non-settled groups, different ethnic groups, or between big and small households.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Near-universal targeting approach (90% coverage) with exclusion only of well-off households based on objective criteria; transparent communications campaign prior to benefit delivery; robust implementation monitoring; grievance redress mechanism; community participatory monitoring; involvement of trusted local leaders (mullahs, elders, teachers); flexible exclusion approach at aggregate rather than community level to avoid single 'affluent household' exclusions in poorer communities; coordination with humanitarian partners to avoid confusion

identified_risks.risk_description: Relief distribution efforts could contribute to COVID-19 spread among workers and beneficiaries. Key staff illness could cause implementation delays. Residual risks from emergency nature of operation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Careful training on social distancing protocols; provision of PPE to all distribution personnel; door-to-door distribution as default modality; designated collection points with social distancing measures; health screening of workers; contingency planning for staff illness; phased implementation approach

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Substantial (project activities have moderate environmental risks but no significant negative environmental impacts expected)
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project will use existing GRMs from CCAP (IDLG and MRRD) and KMDP (KM), customized for COVID-19 circumstances. Mechanisms include: call centers with phone surveys; hotlines for complaints and clarifications; female-friendly complaint channels with female call center operators; grievance resolution at community, district, provincial, and national levels; grievance tracking in MIS; target of 70% of grievances addressed; exploration of independent grievance system available across Afghanistan; community participatory monitoring committees; involvement of trusted local leaders in monitoring
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Comprehensive gender strategy: all female-headed households (FHHs) eligible for support; targeting of 100% of FHHs in project areas; minimum 25% female field staff; female field responders for outreach to women; female CDC members engagement; targeted communications to women through multiple channels (loudspeakers, radio, public announcements); door-to-door delivery for women with limited mobility; suitable delivery times considering women's schedules; female safety measures at distribution sites; separate queues for FHHs where applicable; female representation on CDCs (50% in ENETAWF areas); collection of gender-disaggregated data on household headship; gender-sensitive training for all staff; GBV/SEA prevention measures including Code of Conduct; mobile money accessibility for women; awareness on health risks and hygiene practices targeted to women
vulnerable_groups: Households with no able-bodied male workers

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity: near-universal targeting approach (90% coverage) to minimize exclusion and reduce social tensions; transparent targeting criteria to prevent elite capture; involvement of trusted community leaders (mullahs, elders, teachers) in targeting and monitoring; community participatory monitoring to ensure fairness; grievance redress mechanism to address complaints; coordination with humanitarian partners to avoid confusion and duplication; flexible exclusion approach at aggregate level to avoid creating resentment in poorer communities; clear communications campaign on eligibility and entitlements; coordination with government security forces on crowd management at distribution points; recognition of underlying social tensions and exclusion in conflict-affected context; do-no-harm principles embedded in design
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces multiple fragility factors: ongoing violent conflict with Taliban and insurgent groups; weak state capacity and governance; limited fiscal resources (grants finance 75% of public spending); high poverty rate (55% in 2017, expected to spike to 72% due to COVID-19); limited access to basic services; weak health systems; high food insecurity (12 million people in acute food insecurity); limited financial inclusion (only 15% own transaction accounts); weak institutional capacity; limited social protection systems; displacement and refugee populations; political instability from power-sharing disputes; macroeconomic vulnerabilities; climate shocks (droughts, floods); limited access to information; gender inequality; low human capital index (0.4, bottom quartile globally)
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project operates in context of active conflict and security challenges: Taliban insurgent activity at record highs despite US-Taliban peace agreement; ongoing negotiations between Taliban and Afghan parties with unclear prospects; 47 hard-to-reach districts with limited government access due to security concerns; security risks to project staff and beneficiaries during distribution; need for security force management at distribution points; risk of staff intimidation and violence; coordination with humanitarian partners for HTR areas; phased approach to HTR district coverage; use of facilitating partners with local presence and security awareness; protocols for social distancing and crowd management to prevent clashes with security forces (recent incident in Ghor province); security considerations in cash distribution decisions (physical cash carries higher security risks in rural areas); security screening of staff; contingency planning for security incidents
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project explicitly targets displaced and refugee populations: approximately 1.45 million returnees estimated by IOM since 2017 included in beneficiary estimates; internally displaced persons (IDPs) included in targeting; returnees living in settlement camps inside municipal boundaries of provincial capital cities explicitly mentioned as beneficiaries in Component 2; EZ-Kar project expansion to support internally displaced and former refugee Afghan returnee populations; project design accounts for returnee populations in population estimates and household counts; coordination with IOM on returnee data; inclusion of returnees in household identification and beneficiary lists; special attention to vulnerable returnee households in targeting; support for returnee communities through community-driven development approach
